text: I've been trading the opening range
breakout strategy for the last 6 months
and I've made steady profits with it.
The strategy is simple, but there are
many different versions of it online.
One variant that I see over and over
again is using the orb with a breakout,
pullback, and continuation pattern. It
looks like a really good setup, but the
only way to know if it works is to try
it out. I have back tested 5 years of
S&P 500 data to find out for myself. But
first, I'm just going to do a quick
recap of the way that I trade the
strategy for anyone who's new to the
channel. This is a 15minute opening
range breakout. I'm here on a 15-minute
chart, and I set my time zone to New
York. So, that's UTC minus 4. I then
look for the 9:30 candle, which is the
market open in New York, and that's this
green candle here. Once I've got that,
I'm going to mark out the high and the
low. So, I mark the high and then do the
same thing with the low. This gives me
my opening range. After that, I'm just
waiting for a candle to break out
through this range. There are different
ways to do this, and I know that some
people will enter as soon as the price
is broken, like on this green wick up
here. But I prefer to wait for the
candle to actually break through and
then close above. So, in this case, my
signal is this candle here because it's
the first one to actually break out and
then close outside of that range. I then
enter at the open of the next one. So,
this here would be my entry. And I'll
place my long entry here just to
visualize things a little bit. After
that, the stop loss goes at the bottom
of the range. So down here, and I target
1.5 to one reward to risk. So this
takerit would be somewhere around here.
And this would have been a profitable
trade. There are additional rules that
I've developed like I don't trade after
the 12:00 candle, which would be here.
So that would be my cut off for entering
a trade. And I also don't trade ranges
that are either too small or too big
based on the recent ATR. I'm not going
to go into all of that just now, but
I'll leave a link to the detailed video
where I explain all these rules and I
show the back test as well. The problem
with this approach becomes apparent on
this trade. I've got my 50-minute
opening range marked by this first green
candle, and then I have my breakout, but
the breakout is so big that my entry
ends up being all the way out here. That
in turn leads me to have a really big
stop-loss on my trade because I place
the stop at the bottom of the range. But
often what seems to happen after the
initial breakout is that we get this
pullback towards the opening range. This
is how the modified strategy will work.
First, I switch over to the 5-minut
chart. This way, I can react to price
action a lot quicker. My orb range is
still defined by the first 15 minutes of
the day. But since I'm on the 5-minut
time frame now, I have to use the first
three candles to define my range high
and range low. Once I have those
defined, I then wait for my breakout
just like before. But this time, I don't
buy immediately on the close of this
breakout candle. Instead, I have to wait
and see if price is going to make a
pullback towards the opening range. And
this will be difficult psychologically
because I'll be getting FOMO about
missing out on a potential trade,
especially if it continues going up the
way. But according to this strategy,
price will often make a pullback and it
will return back to the orb. And what
I'm looking for is a candle that's going
to break through the range but then
close back above it, which is exactly
what this candle here has done. The
reason for this is that I want to see
buyers coming back in and pushing the
price back up. That gives me
confirmation that this line is now
starting to act as support. Once I get
this pullback candle, I don't enter
immediately. I want to see confirmation
that price is going to bounce off and
move back up. And I'm going to be
waiting for a break of this most recent
high. We get that on the next candle.
And this gives me my confirmation to
enter into a trade. So, the trade is
opened here. The stop loss will still go
at the bottom of my opening range. And I
will target 1 and 1/2 times for my
take-profit ratio. I don't have to put
the stop loss down here. It could also
go at the bottom of this pullback
candle. And likewise, I don't have to
always aim for one and a half times. And
I will be testing out all of these
different scenarios. And this completes
the breakout, pullback, and continuation
pattern. The theory here is pretty
simple, but it requires patience not to
chase that initial move, but to actually
wait for price to come back down and
give a better entry. I jumped over to my
back tester and started setting this up,
but it actually turned out to be a lot
more complicated than I thought. And by
the way, I'm happy to share this back
test code. Let me know in the comments
if you want to see the code and I can
share the link to the GitHub repo. The
first challenge that I had with this
system was how do I actually define a
breakout because this here is obviously
a breakout, but then so is this. Price
has broken out and closed above the
opening range. But I would trust this
breakout far less than I would trust
this one. Visually, it's easy to see the
difference, but for my back tester, I
need to take into account the height of
this breakout. And I made sure that this
was greater than at least 1 times ATR to
be considered a suitable breakout. I
then configured the pullback and
continuation logic, but I regularly had
to check my simulated trade log and fix
any bugs that I found. Finally, I was
happy with the accuracy of my back test,
and this was the result over those 5
years of testing. The strategy seemed to
perform okay for the last 2 years from
2023 onwards. But before that, it was
pretty much sideways for the first 3
years. Going by the equity curve alone,
I really wasn't impressed with this
performance. Looking at the stats here,
the win rate is actually very similar to
what my current strategy does. I
expected that waiting for a pullback to
get a better price would actually give
me a better win rate, but it doesn't
seem to have made much of a difference.
What it has done, though, is reduce the
overall number of trades. So now over
that 5-year period, I only got 130
trades with this system, and the overall
return for the strategy was pretty poor.
So, it wasn't off to the best start, but
then I thought maybe the 1 and a half
times ratio was too restrictive. The
entries in theory are much better now,
so I should be able to aim for bigger
profits. I reran the simulation and this
time I tested take-profit ratios
starting at 2 1/2 and going all the way
up to 4 1/2 in increments of 0.5. And it
seems that each increment made the
strategy perform that little bit better.
So, it certainly was an improvement, but
I would still say that the equity curve
overall for all of these is still pretty
poor. and the final balance is far less
than what my current strategy does. Then

giving me the initial opening range high

However, the initial pullback broke all

So that invalidated the trade. I waited

through into the opening range and then
it was rejected pretty strongly
indicating that this has now turned into

did actually work out. It went in the

So, the entry level between the two
strategies is practically identical. The
whole point of this breakout pullback
continuation is that I should get a

now. So, maybe what I'm doing wrong is
I'm waiting too long to get back into
the trade. Instead, should I just place
a stop order at the opening range? That
way, when price hits it, I get in
immediately for that bounce back up.
Well, I tested that, too. This time, the
stop loss is still at the bottom of the
range. I have a 1.5 take profit, but now
I'm using a stop order at the top of the
orb. And the results here are even
worse. Without the confirmation that
price has made the pullback and is now
continuing back up, I'm losing far more
trades. And as a result, the performance
is awful. But I wasn't ready to give up
yet. I noticed something else in these
examples, which is that when price came
back down to the orb, quite often it
actually moved quite far into it,
somewhere around the halfway point
before bouncing back up. So maybe it's
not the high of the opening range that I
should be focused on, but it's actually
the midpoint. And this is what it would
look like. I'm still measuring the
breakout from the top of the opening
range, but now a pullback down to the
opening range isn't enough. I'm waiting
for price to pull back all the way into
this midpoint here, and that's where I'm
going to buy with a stop order. I'm not
looking for confirmation of a previous
high being broken. I just enter
immediately as it reaches the midpoint.
I kept seeing this happen over and over
again. Oftent times almost to the pip,
the price would retrace back into that
midpoint, bounce really strongly back
up. The additional benefit here is that
because I'm at the midpoint, my stop
loss is far smaller. So, I don't need as
much movement back up to get my one and
a half times or I could aim for even
more. I was excited about this one and I
really thought I was on to something.
So, then I tested it and this was the
result. Somehow, this performed even

for every one trade where this worked
out, there's probably plenty others
where price just barreled all the way
through, easily taking me out. By this
point, I had spent many hours testing
all these different variations, and none
of them were showing profitable results.
I didn't want to waste time on something
that clearly wasn't working, so I
decided to stop testing this any
further. As disappointing as those
results were, I think I know why they
didn't work, and I have an idea on how
to fix it. The first problem, I think,
is that many of the screenshots that we
see in all of these videos that promote
the strategy are these perfect
cherrypicked examples. They make the
setup look really simple and reliable,
but as I've shown, the majority of
trades don't look like this at all. Most
of the time, the breakout isn't all that
clean, or the pullback is confirmed, but
price moves the wrong way anyway. But I
think most importantly, the best
breakouts are the ones that have strong
momentum, and they don't often pull back
down to the opening range. They might
have small corrections along the way,
but nothing significant. This is an
example of a real trade that my bot took
and the entry was down here. As soon as
the bot entered the trade, price just
moved immediately towards the takerit.
By sitting and waiting for a pullback,
I'm essentially skipping out on the
strongest breakouts, trading only those
that were weak enough to come back down
to the opening range. And I think that's
the key reason my back test here failed.
And this flips the whole thing with
pullback and continuation on its head a
little bit, at least in the context of
the opening range. And this back test
gave me another idea, which is actually
to do the opposite of waiting for a
pullback and instead focus on the
momentum and the strength of the initial
breakout to see if it's possible to
avoid some of the fake outs and catch
the strongest breakouts instead. I can
